steps.title: Find the key signal in the passage
steps.content: The passage concludes that sellers benefit by prompting consumers to “consider their own valuations.” The blank must illustrate this benefit using the graph data. Look for which messaging condition produced the highest prices—that would show how prompting value consideration drives revenue.

steps.title: Match the messaging effect to the graph
steps.content:
The “pay what you think it's worth” message directly prompts consumers to consider value. In the graph, this condition produces the highest mean prices for both groups: \$54 for the online sample and \$74 for students. In contrast, “pay what you can” and “pay what you want” produce lower prices (roughly \$27–39).

Choice C states that both groups “tended to choose a higher price when considering the value of the tickets than when considering what they could afford or wanted to pay”—exactly this pattern. This demonstrates the passage's claim: explicitly asking consumers to consider value yields higher prices than other prompts. The other three choices either reverse the price relationships, ignore the messaging conditions, or focus on group differences rather than how the messaging itself works.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
